does: This paper proposes Diff-Instruct with Diffused Reward (DIDR), a reinforcement learning framework for optimizing one-step text-to-image generators. The core contribution addresses a fundamental mismatch in existing approaches that optimize image-space rewards while performing distribution matching in diffusion noise-space, which causes optimization to exploit stochastic degrees of freedom and degrade image quality. DIDR derives from Integral KL minimization principles and propagates an RLHF-optimal reward-tilted distribution across the entire diffusion trajectory from clean images to noise. The method introduces the Diffused Reward Score (DRS), which acts as a reward correction to the reference score function, enabling principled trajectory-level alignment without requiring additional data. Empirical results demonstrate that DIDR improves both reward optimization and image fidelity compared to prior one-step generator RL methods.
connects: This work builds on recent advances in efficient text-to-image generation through one-step diffusion models, which have accelerated synthesis from iterative sampling to real-time inference. It directly addresses limitations in prior RL approaches for generators that combined image-space reward optimization with diffusion-space distribution matching, extending methods from RLHF applied to vision models. The paper connects to foundational work on diffusion-based generative modeling and score-function learning, as well as trajectory-level RL optimization principles. It responds to the specific challenge of reward hacking in generative models, where optimization exploits stochasticity rather than improving genuine generation quality. The Integral KL minimization framework draws on principled probabilistic optimization theory to bridge the gap between terminal reward optimization and the continuous generative dynamics underlying diffusion processes.
enables: DIDR establishes a principled framework for reward optimization in one-step generators that could extend to other efficient generation paradigms and control objectives. The diffused reward perspective suggests new directions for understanding and controlling trade-offs between reward maximization and sample quality across the generative trajectory. This work could enable more reliable human-feedback optimization for visual models, reducing reward hacking in alignment scenarios. Future work might apply diffused rewards to iterative diffusion refinement, extend the framework to other modalities, or develop efficient approximations for scaling to larger models. The principled trajectory-level alignment could also inform approaches to controllable generation and multi-objective optimization in diffusion models, potentially leading to more robust alignment methods for large-scale generative AI systems.
